refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable operators and the system to achieve and maintain energy efficiency performance such that measured energy_per_ton (energy_used / ton_moved) does not exceed the customer-defined E_target over the agreed evaluation period, under nominal operating conditions and across the range of supported worksite scenarios.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-003 as stated is a KPI support requirement but lacks specificity on measurement scope, boundary conditions, and operational context. Refinement clarifies that energy efficiency is a measurable outcome dependent on operator behavior, vehicle efficiency, teleoperation overhead, and safety system energy draw. The refined FR establishes the acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target) as the verification anchor and acknowledges that multiple subsystems contribute to this outcome.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Establish a baseline energy consumption profile across representative worksite scenarios (load classes, terrain, distance, cycle time). Measure total energy input (fuel or electrical) and total mass moved (ton) over the evaluation period. Calculate energy_per_ton and compare against E_target. Disaggregate energy consumption by subsystem (propulsion, teleoperation, safety services, auxiliary) to identify efficiency drivers and bottlenecks.
sub_frs: FR-EE-001: The vehicle propulsion system shall minimize energy consumption per unit distance traveled under load.
FR-EE-002: The teleoperation subsystem shall minimize energy overhead (communication, processing, latency compensation) relative to direct operation.
FR-EE-003: The safety services subsystem shall execute safety-critical functions without exceeding a defined energy budget.
FR-EE-004: The NGHE solution shall provide real-time energy consumption visibility and efficiency metrics to the operator and site manager.
FR-EE-005: The NGHE solution shall support operator training and behavior optimization to achieve energy efficiency targets.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement a closed-loop energy management system that monitors real-time energy consumption across all subsystems, compares against a dynamic efficiency baseline, and provides corrective feedback to the operator and autonomous control systems to optimize energy per ton.

DP-B: Optimize vehicle drivetrain efficiency (engine/motor, transmission, hydraulics) through component selection and control tuning; independently optimize teleoperation and safety subsystem energy budgets; aggregate subsystem efficiency targets to meet overall E_target.

DP-C: Establish a hierarchical energy governance model: define energy budgets for vehicle, teleoperation, and safety subsystems; allocate E_target proportionally; implement local energy optimization within each subsystem; monitor and report aggregate energy_per_ton against E_target.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-C: Establish a hierarchical energy governance model with subsystem-level energy budgets (vehicle propulsion, teleoperation, safety services) that sum to the overall E_target. Each subsystem optimizes within its allocated budget. The NGHE solution monitors aggregate energy consumption and ton_moved, calculates energy_per_ton in real time, and reports against E_target. This approach enables independent subsystem optimization, clear accountability, and modular verification.

potential_sources: Teleoperation energy overhead is coupled to vehicle propulsion efficiency (latency compensation may require more aggressive control, increasing energy draw).
Safety services energy draw is coupled to operational tempo (higher cycle rates increase safety system activation frequency).
Operator behavior (acceleration, idle time, route selection) couples all subsystems and dominates energy_per_ton variance.
coupling_check.notes: DP-C mitigates coupling by establishing explicit energy budgets and monitoring aggregate outcome (energy_per_ton). Coupling is managed through feedback and operator training (FR-EE-005), not eliminated. This is acceptable because energy efficiency is inherently a system-level property.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: E_target is defined and stable for the evaluation period. Risk: If E_target is revised mid-evaluation, subsystem budgets must be re-allocated, potentially invalidating prior optimization work.

Assumption: Energy consumption can be accurately measured across all subsystems. Risk: Measurement instrumentation may have gaps (e.g., hydraulic losses, auxiliary loads), leading to underestimation of actual energy draw.
Assumption: Operator behavior can be influenced through feedback and training. Risk: Operator compliance with efficiency guidance may be low if it conflicts with productivity or safety perception.
Assumption: Teleoperation and safety subsystems have well-defined, stable energy profiles. Risk: If these subsystems are immature or subject to frequent changes, energy budgets may be unrealistic.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-C to explicitly define the energy measurement boundary (what is included/excluded), the frequency and granularity of energy_per_ton reporting, and the mechanism for operator feedback and behavior change. Clarify whether E_target is a hard constraint or a target with tolerance bands. Define escalation procedures if aggregate energy_per_ton exceeds E_target during evaluation.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution is the top-level integration module that orchestrates energy governance across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services subsystems. It owns the energy measurement infrastructure, energy_per_ton calculation, reporting to stakeholders, and feedback mechanisms to optimize operator behavior. The NGHE Solution does not own the internal optimization of individual subsystems but coordinates their energy budgets and monitors aggregate performance.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution receives energy consumption data (kWh or fuel volume, ton_moved, cycle metrics) from Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services subsystems via Telemetry. NGHE Solution outputs energy_per_ton KPI, efficiency alerts, and operator guidance via Telemetry to Site Manager and Equipment Operator dashboards.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution receives E_target definition, evaluation period parameters, and energy budget allocation directives from Site Manager via Tasking interface.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution may issue SafetyAlert if energy_per_ton degradation indicates a potential safety issue (e.g., insufficient energy for emergency braking or safety system activation).

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution uses Network Link to communicate energy metrics, operator feedback, and efficiency reports to cloud-based analytics and Site Manager systems.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the natural owner of energy governance, measurement aggregation, and KPI reporting. Energy efficiency is a system-level property that requires cross-subsystem visibility and coordination. Minimal diffusion: energy measurement instrumentation is distributed across subsystems, but aggregation logic and reporting are centralized in NGHE Solution.

Vehicle owns propulsion efficiency optimization (DP-C subsystem budget) but does not own overall energy governance or cross-subsystem coordination. If energy_per_ton logic were placed in Vehicle, it would require Vehicle to know about Teleoperation and Safety Services energy budgets, creating inappropriate coupling and diffusion.

Teleoperation owns its own energy budget optimization but is not responsible for overall energy governance. Placing energy_per_ton logic in Teleoperation would create high diffusion and inappropriate responsibility for Vehicle and Safety Services efficiency.

Safety Services owns its energy budget but is not responsible for overall energy governance or cross-subsystem coordination. Placing energy efficiency KPI logic in Safety Services would violate separation of concerns and create diffusion.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: NGHE Solution is the appropriate owner because: (1) energy efficiency is a system-level KPI that requires aggregation across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services; (2) NGHE Solution already owns stakeholder communication (Site Manager, Operator) and is the natural point for reporting energy_per_ton; (3) energy governance (budget allocation, feedback, escalation) is a cross-subsystem concern that belongs at the system level; (4) this assignment minimizes diffusion and maintains clear separation of concerns.
containment_risks: Risk: NGHE Solution may become a bottleneck if energy measurement and reporting are not efficiently implemented. Mitigation: Use asynchronous telemetry collection and batch reporting to avoid real-time performance impact.

Risk: If Vehicle, Teleoperation, or Safety Services do not provide accurate energy consumption data via Telemetry, NGHE Solution cannot calculate energy_per_ton correctly. Mitigation: Define energy measurement standards and validation procedures for each subsystem.

Risk: Operator feedback and behavior change mechanisms may be ineffective if not well-integrated with operator workflow. Mitigation: Coordinate with Equipment Operator and Tele-operator stakeholders to design feedback mechanisms that are actionable and non-intrusive.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Clarify the energy measurement instrumentation strategy: which subsystems provide energy consumption data, at what granularity and frequency, and via which Telemetry channels. Define the energy_per_ton calculation algorithm (e.g., rolling average, per-cycle, per-shift) and the reporting cadence. Establish data validation and error handling procedures to ensure energy_per_ton is reliable and trustworthy.

fr_dp_gate.reason: The refined FR clearly states the energy efficiency acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target) and the evaluation scope. DP-C (hierarchical energy governance with subsystem budgets) directly addresses this by establishing a measurement and feedback system that enables the NGHE solution to achieve and maintain the target. The DP is logically sound and does not introduce unnecessary complexity. The mapping is one-to-many (one FR to five sub-FRs), which is appropriate for a system-level KPI that depends on multiple subsystems.

diffusion_risks: Energy measurement instrumentation is distributed across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services, but aggregation is centralized in NGHE Solution. This is acceptable because it maintains clear ownership boundaries while enabling system-level visibility.

Operator feedback mechanisms may require integration with Vehicle and Teleoperation interfaces (e.g., dashboard, alerts). Ensure that feedback is coordinated through NGHE Solution to avoid conflicting or redundant messages.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the appropriate owner of energy governance and KPI reporting. The assignment is clean: NGHE Solution owns the system-level logic, while Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services own their respective subsystem optimizations. External interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and do not introduce unexpected dependencies.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Energy_per_ton can be verified by measuring total energy consumption (via Telemetry from subsystems) and total ton_moved (via Vehicle odometry and load sensors). The calculation is straightforward and can be automated. Verification is feasible if energy measurement instrumentation is accurate and complete.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: The refined FR is allocated to NGHE Solution with clear sub-FRs for Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. Each subsystem has a defined energy budget and optimization objective. Allocation is clear and traceable.

recommended_revisions: Define the energy measurement boundary explicitly: what energy sources are included (fuel, electrical, hydraulic losses), what are excluded (e.g., auxiliary loads not directly related to material movement), and how are they measured.
Specify the energy_per_ton calculation algorithm: is it a rolling average, per-cycle, per-shift, or per-evaluation-period? What is the reporting frequency?
Clarify the operator feedback mechanism: how is energy_per_ton information presented to the Equipment Operator and Tele-operator? What actions can they take to improve efficiency?
Define escalation procedures: if energy_per_ton exceeds E_target during evaluation, what corrective actions are triggered? Who is responsible for investigation and remediation?

decision.justification: The refined FR is well-formed and the DP-IM assignment is sound. However, the FR is not atomic; it decomposes into five sub-FRs (FR-EE-001 through FR-EE-005) that require further refinement and DP selection. The next level of decomposition is necessary to define how each subsystem (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) will optimize within its energy budget and how operator behavior will be influenced. Additionally, the energy measurement and reporting infrastructure (FR-EE-004) requires detailed design to ensure accuracy and usability.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-EE-001: The vehicle propulsion system shall minimize energy consumption per unit distance traveled under load.
FR-EE-002: The teleoperation subsystem shall minimize energy overhead (communication, processing, latency compensation) relative to direct operation.
FR-EE-003: The safety services subsystem shall execute safety-critical functions without exceeding a defined energy budget.
FR-EE-004: The NGHE solution shall provide real-time energy consumption visibility and efficiency metrics to the operator and site manager.
FR-EE-005: The NGHE solution shall support operator training and behavior optimization to achieve energy efficiency targets.

assumptions: E_target is defined and stable for the evaluation period.
Energy consumption can be accurately measured across all subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services).
Operator behavior can be influenced through feedback and training to improve energy efficiency.
Teleoperation and safety subsystems have well-defined, stable energy profiles.
Energy efficiency is a measurable outcome that can be aggregated across subsystems without loss of fidelity.
The NGHE Solution has access to real-time telemetry from all subsystems and can calculate energy_per_ton with acceptable latency.
open_risks: Energy measurement instrumentation may have gaps or inaccuracies, leading to unreliable energy_per_ton calculations.
Operator compliance with efficiency guidance may be low if it conflicts with productivity or safety perception.
Teleoperation energy overhead may be difficult to isolate and optimize independently of Vehicle propulsion efficiency.
Safety services energy draw may be unpredictable if safety-critical events are rare or scenario-dependent.
E_target may be revised mid-evaluation, requiring re-allocation of subsystem budgets and invalidating prior optimization work.
Feedback mechanisms may be ineffective if not well-integrated with operator workflow and decision-making.
evidence_needed: Baseline energy consumption profile across representative worksite scenarios (load classes, terrain, distance, cycle time).
Energy measurement instrumentation specifications and validation procedures for each subsystem.
E_target definition and acceptance criteria (hard constraint vs. target with tolerance bands).
Operator feedback mechanism design and usability testing results.
Teleoperation energy overhead characterization (communication, processing, latency compensation).
Safety services energy budget and allocation methodology.
Evaluation period definition and energy_per_ton reporting cadence.
